title: Character Visual Mode
subtitle: {key:v} — select first, act second.
summary: v enters character-wise Visual mode. Move to extend the selection. Press an operator (d, c, y, …) to act on it.

blocks.text: Visual mode flips the grammar around. Instead of "verb then noun" ({key:d}{key:w}), you do "noun then verb": select first, then act. {key:v} starts the selection, motions extend it, and an operator finishes the job.

blocks.label: Switch which end of the selection moves

blocks.text: Press {key:Esc} or {key:v} again to leave Visual mode without acting. Press an operator to act and return to Normal.

blocks.title: Visual Is Just an Anchored Cursor
blocks.text:
When you press {key:v}, Vim sets an **anchor** at the cursor and remembers it. Every motion now updates the *cursor* while the *anchor* stays put. The selection is the (anchor → cursor) range — and Vim keeps it sorted so the lower position is always the start.

{key:o} *swaps* the anchor and cursor. So if you started a selection going forward and realize you need to extend it backward, {key:o} flips which end your motions move.

When you finally press an operator, Vim hands the operator the (start, end) pair just like a motion would. **Visual mode and operator+motion are the same thing internally** — they just package the range differently.

blocks.title: The Last Visual Selection Is Remembered
blocks.text:
Whenever you exit Visual mode, Vim sets two automatic marks: {key:`}{key:<} (start) and {key:`}{key:>} (end). They live in the buffer until your next visual selection.

This means:

  • {key:g}{key:v}  re-selects the last visual range.
  • {key::}{key:'}{key:<}{key:,}{key:'}{key:>} (or just {key::} from Visual) targets it as an Ex range — useful for {key::}{key:s}{key:/}{key:foo}{key:/}{key:bar}{key:/}{key:g}.

Most "do an Ex command on what I just selected" idioms work this way. See [`visual.range`](#visual.range).

blocks.title: Visual mode is anchor + endpoint
blocks.text: Charwise visual mode stores two positions: the anchor (where you pressed {key:v}) and the current cursor. Motions move the cursor; the selection is always the inclusive range between the two. There's no selection object being copied around — the renderer paints whatever sits between anchor and cursor each frame. Operators consume the resolved range and exit back to normal mode.
